mnemonics: Remember 'GRADient is a VECTOR' - Gradients are vectors, so their operations (like dot/cross product) follow vector rules, not scalar ones.
keyPoints: The gradient of a sum is the sum of the gradients: ∇(f + g) = ∇f + ∇g.
The product rule: ∇(fg) = f(∇g) + g(∇f). The gradient operates on a product of two scalar functions.
The chain rule: ∇(f(g)) = f'(g) ∇g. The derivative of the outer function times the gradient of the inner function.
commonMistakes: Applying the scalar product rule incorrectly to the dot product of two gradients, e.g., ∇(∇f · ∇g) is NOT (∇f)(∇·∇g) + (∇g)(∇·∇f).
Forgetting that the gradient is a linear operator and trying to distribute it over a product without using the product rule: ∇(fg) ≠ (∇f)(∇g).
